era_context: The mid-14th century in Europe was marked by the aftermath of the Black Death (1347–1353), which had killed 30–60% of the continent's population. Political fragmentation characterized the period: the Hundred Years' War between England and France continued; the Holy Roman Empire remained decentralized; and Italian city-states competed for regional dominance. In Asia, the Ming Dynasty was consolidating power in China after overthrowing the Mongol Yuan; the Ottoman Empire was expanding in Anatolia and the Balkans; and the Delhi Sultanate controlled much of the Indian subcontinent. This was an era of demographic recovery, religious reform movements, and the early stirrings of what would become the Renaissance.
geographic_coverage_gaps: Sub-Saharan Africa (limited written sources from this period)
Southeast Asia (sparse European documentation; local chronicles less accessible)
Mesoamerica and South America (pre-Columbian records fragmentary)

events.title: Murad I becomes Ottoman Sultan
events.description: Murad I (r. 1361–1389) ascends to the Ottoman throne following the death of his father Orhan I. Murad I is remembered as the architect of Ottoman military expansion into the Balkans and the consolidation of Ottoman state institutions, particularly the Janissary corps. His reign marked the transformation of the Ottomans from a regional Anatolian beylik into a major imperial power that would eventually dominate the Eastern Mediterranean and Balkans for centuries.

sources.name: Ottoman chronicles (Aşıkpaşazade, Neşri); later historiographic consensus
Byzantine sources (contemporary references to Ottoman expansion)

events.certainty_note: Ottoman succession dates are well-attested in both Ottoman and Byzantine sources. Murad I's accession in 1361 is accepted by modern Ottoman historiography.
cross_references: 1389-001 (Battle of Kosovo, end of Murad I's reign)

events.title: Peasant Revolt in Flanders
events.description: A major uprising of Flemish peasants and urban workers, known as the Peasants' Revolt or Révolte des Paysans, erupts in response to heavy taxation and feudal oppression. The revolt is brutally suppressed by the nobility, but it reflects widespread social discontent across northern Europe in the post-plague era, when labor scarcity had emboldened the lower classes to demand better conditions. The uprising is part of a broader pattern of 14th-century social unrest.
events.certainty_note: The Flemish Peasant Revolt of 1361 is well-documented in contemporary chronicles and municipal archives. Dating is secure.
cross_references: 1381-001 (Peasants' Revolt in England, similar social dynamics)

events.title: Zhu Yuanzhang consolidates Ming control in southern China
events.description: Zhu Yuanzhang, who would found the Ming Dynasty, continues his military campaigns to eliminate rival warlords and consolidate control over southern China. By 1361, he has established his base at Nanjing and is systematically reducing competing power centers. This period marks the final phase of Yuan Dynasty collapse and the rise of Ming hegemony, which would last until 1644.

sources.name: Ming Shilu (Ming Veritable Records, compiled later but based on contemporary documents)

events.certainty_note: Zhu Yuanzhang's military campaigns in the 1360s are well-documented in Chinese historical records. The consolidation process is dated with reasonable precision.
cross_references: 1368-001 (Zhu Yuanzhang proclaims himself Hongwu Emperor; Ming Dynasty formally established)

events.title: Vijayanagara Empire consolidates power in southern India
events.description: The Vijayanagara Empire, founded in 1336, continues to expand and consolidate its control over southern India under the Sangama dynasty. By 1361, the empire has become a major regional power, rivaling the Delhi Sultanate in the north. The empire is known for its administrative efficiency, military innovation, and patronage of Hindu culture and architecture.

sources.name: Portuguese and Arab merchant accounts (later, but based on contemporary knowledge)

events.certainty_note: Vijayanagara's expansion in the 1360s is attested in inscriptions and temple records, though specific events for 1361 are not always precisely dated. The broader trajectory is secure.

disconfirming_evidence: The dating of Murad I's accession has been subject to minor scholarly debate; some sources place it in 1362, though 1361 is the consensus date. The Flemish Peasant Revolt of 1361 is sometimes conflated with earlier or later uprisings in the region; the specific chronology depends on which sources are prioritized. Zhu Yuanzhang's exact military movements in 1361 are difficult to pinpoint to a single year, as campaigns often spanned multiple years and sources sometimes compress or expand timelines for narrative effect.
historiographic_note: Documentation for 1361 is uneven. European sources (particularly Froissart and Flemish records) are rich for Western Europe. Ottoman and Chinese sources are substantial but often compiled after the fact. Indian sources are primarily epigraphic and architectural, which limits precision for specific years. Sub-Saharan Africa, Southeast Asia, and the Americas are largely absent from surviving written records for this year, reflecting both the limitations of literacy and the Eurocentric bias of modern historiography.

graph_edges.note: Murad I's reign (1361–1389) culminates in the Battle of Kosovo
Part of broader 14th-century peasant unrest across Europe
Zhu Yuanzhang's consolidation leads to formal Ming Dynasty proclamation
